preamble: Rhotacism is a phonetic process where an intervocalic consonant (typically [s] or [z]) changes into a rhotic consonant [r]. This famously occurred in Archaic Latin, where intervocalic /s/ first voiced to [z] then shifted to [r], and in Proto-Germanic via Verner's Law.

examples.note: Stem-final *flosis → floris; the intervocalic /s/ voiced then rhoticized.
Intervocalic /s/ of the stem became /r/ before the genitive suffix -is.
Verner's Law voiced PIE *s to *z in the plural; subsequent rhotacism yielded *wērun > were.

sources: Roberts, P. (2012). Latin rhotacism: a case study in the life cycle of phonological processes. Transactions of the Philological Society, 110(1), 80–93. https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1467-968X.2012.01285.x
